items.title: I packaged up the "autoresearch" project into a new self-contained minimal repo if people would like...
items.content:
I packaged up the "autoresearch" project into a new self-contained minimal repo if people would like to play over the weekend. It's basically nanochat LLM training core stripped down to a single-GPU, one file version of ~630 lines of code, then:

- the human iterates on the prompt (.md)
- the AI agent iterates on the training code (.py)

The goal is to engineer your agents to make the fastest research progress indefinitely and without any of your own involvement. In the image, every dot is a complete LLM training run that lasts exactly 5 minutes. The agent works in an autonomous loop on a git feature branch and accumulates git commits to the training script as it finds better settings (of lower validation loss by the end) of the neural network architecture, the optimizer, all the hyperparameters, etc. You can imagine comparing the research progress of different prompts, different agents, etc.

https://t.co/YCvOwwjOzF
Part code, part sci-fi, and a pinch of psychosis :)

items.title: Another unsolved (& admittedly hard) AI benchmark: "write a satisfying 10 paragraph murder mystery. ...
items.content:
Another unsolved (& admittedly hard) AI benchmark: "write a satisfying 10 paragraph murder mystery. the pieces you need to solve the mystery should be clear enough in the first five paragraphs that you could solve it, but obscure enough that the vast majority of people will not"

Errors are revealing:
-Claude forgets to add the actual clue to the puzzle (and the details are too obscure), a classic planning problem for LLMs, and no, using Cowork or Code doesn't help. 

-ChatGPT 5.4 Pro creates a completely obvious clue and then proceeds to write with the over-elaborate metaphors and complications that have haunted ChatGPT fiction. Pro did better than Thinking,  though.

-Gemini 3.1 Pro is closest, but the ice is a little obvious, and it completely flubs the explanation about why the ice thing was important.

It takes zero energy to stay certain of your current thesis. Meanwhile curiosity takes a lot of energy and discomfort. It requires constantly disassembling and rebuilding your world model.

That's what makes certainty so dangerous: it's the bottom of the potential well and it's hard to get out.

items.content:
Anthropic just made it free to run your own AI builder meetups, anywhere on Earth.

They launched Claude Community Ambassadors, a global program to grow local builder communities city by city.

> Open to any background
> Free event sponsorship, API credits, swag
> Multiple ambassadors per city allowed
> Direct partnership with the Anthropic team

Here's how it works: apply, do a short screening call, sign the agreement, join a private Slack, and start planning your first event.

No cost to you.

Until now, grassroots AI communities had zero institutional backing. You'd organize meetups on your own, with no resources and no direct line to the team building the tools.

This flips that. You can now host hackathons and workshops with real funding, get API credits to share with attendees, and feed feedback directly to the people shipping the product.

https://t.co/jYofsI6dJf

@debgotwired @0xPaulius Medium is a better default for most tasks. Think of effort as “max effort” — the model may decide to use less effort if it wants, and can use up to the amount of effort you set it to. It’s a balance-

Low: fast and less intelligent
Medium: balanced
High: very intelligent, can be slow and token-intensive for some tasks

items.content:
If Iran put a missile into the San Francisco backyard I just left a good number of robotics companies would cease to exist.

I am one lucky mf’er.

One guy stood out from the rest.

Found a way to get a unique dataset that his competition will find it hard to get to. Took him six years to get it himself and he has to work many deals in India and elsewhere to get it.

His model can enable a robot to figure out how to run any computer faster than any other.

Not one of these guys, there was a big crowd behind me of people who build all sorts of robots from agricultural ones to industrial robots of various kinds (I was one of three people who weren’t running a robot company). I just took a picture because this event might be worthy of a new Silicon Valley TV show: https://t.co/yP5ncjwUBH

Except it is San Francisco.

It is an amazing place building the future.

The Chicken had Indian spices. Was so good.

Thanks @p_mohansivam for a very special night (that is him with the fun glasses).

i'm guessing: 
WINDOW_PATTERN = "L"
is a lot...
items.content:
@miolini runs great but probably requires some tuning!

i'm guessing: 
WINDOW_PATTERN = "L"
is a lot faster (mixed window sizes are only natively supported by FA3)

then problem:
DEPTH a lot lower, e.g. even 4?
DEVICE_BATCH_SIZE can probably go up more then
TOTAL_BATCH_SIZE probably a lot lower, e.g. 2**16? 

needs a bit of tuning to get to a better initial spot (or you can try to let the agent figure it out, but it's not certain it would. could be fun to try!).

items.title:
A @waymo predicts what you are about to do then drives around where you will be.

FactSim sounds hyp...
items.content:
A @waymo predicts what you are about to do then drives around where you will be.

FactSim sounds hyper weird when you first hear about it, but simulated lives could warn us about dating choices we are making, for instance.

My first wife hated tech and I thought I could convert her. After we split up 24 years ago she ended up buying a ranch with horses. 

Maybe having such a thing would have kept me from making mistakes.

But maybe that is, indeed, a bad thing. 

The craziness brought me to Maryam. If I had some way to convince my brain not to do crazy things I wouldn’t have the stories I do today.

Be less painful for sure to improve yourself..

It’s better intelligence.

This robot understands space and task flow instead of following rigid scripts.

→ Adapt in real time
→ Stay safe around humans

That’s how you move from lab demos to real deployments.

→ They optimize for how robots look, not how they work

Hotels are edge cases all day long.

Furniture moves daily.
Humans leave obstacles everywhere.

items.title: @ReadOnlyDude1 @ChapterPal There have been conversion issues recently. I fixed them. Are you using P...

items.reasoning: Minor insight about LLM vision capability improvements enabling new product features, but mostly product support.
items.summary_html: Burkov explains ChapterPal's PDF reader feature, noting modern LLMs are much better at understanding images, enabling direct PDF reading without markdown conversion.
items.content_html:
@ReadOnlyDude1 @ChapterPal There have been conversion issues recently. I fixed them. Are you using Premium OCR or the regular one? Premium provides much better output quality.

The new PDF reader is available on higher plans too. Just upload a document and click on the "Start reading now" link in the conversion progress bar. I'm working on making the PDF reader available in a more intuitive way on higher plans, but if you don't like to read in Markdown, you don't need to pay for a higher plan, IMO.

The quality of AI answers when reading as PDF shouldn't be much worse. The difference with the converted books is that when you read as an unconverted PDF, the LLM sees the images and not the text, but modern LLMs are much better at understanding images than they were a year ago. This is why I decided to add this feature now and not then.

items.title:
A new paid Reader plan on @ChapterPal. It's "PDF only" for $5.99/month.

The plan includes 20 monthl...
items.content:
A new paid Reader plan on @ChapterPal. It's "PDF only" for $5.99/month.

The plan includes 20 monthly PDF uploads and it doesn't do PDF to markdown conversion default on other plans.

The new PDF reader looks very much like the original conversational reader except that instead of gradually revealing markdown sentences and paragraphs, the user reveals parts of the PDF: paragraphs, figures, tables, and equations.

Because there's no PDF to markdown conversion, getting to reading the uploaded document is much faster, and the backend cost for me is substantially less.

$5.99/month is only $1 above what Adobe charges for its AI assistant in Acrobat, but 1) the ChapterPal UX is way better, 2) includes access to a large library of high-quality books and curricula, and 3) who really wants to give Adobe money?

Mermaid Live (Mermaid Live Editor) is a free, browser-ba...
items.content:
I asked Google AI to explain Mermaid Live…

Mermaid Live (Mermaid Live Editor) is a free, browser-based application used to create, edit, and preview diagrams—such as flowcharts, sequence diagrams, and Gantt charts—in real-time. It utilizes Markdown-inspired text to generate visuals without requiring software installation. 
 
Key features of the Mermaid Live Editor include:
•Real-time Rendering: Instantly visualize diagrams as you type code in a split-pane editor.
•Variety of Diagrams: Supports flowcharts, sequence, class, state, and Gantt diagrams.
•Export Options: Allows exporting of diagrams.
•Open Source: It is an open-source tool, often used for quick diagramming and prototyping.
•Integration: The generated Mermaid code can be easily embedded in Markdown files (e.g., in GitHub or Obsidian). ￼  
The tool is accessible at https://t.co/76KwVAclnZ

@ReadOnlyDude1 @ChapterPal If I don't make it more expensive based on the underlying cost, I will go bankrupt :-) The higher plans are for higher consumption. Every page converted into markdown costs something.

If the user doesn't need markdown, there's now the Reader plan. I will later add pay as you go credits for those who need to upload more pages on the Reader plan. The Reader feature will also be made available on higher plans.
